ur:Master robotics from sensors to intel: Master robotics from sensors to intelligent systems
ur:- where artificial intelligence meets: - where artificial intelligence meets the physical world through robotics and autonomous systems.
ur:Physical AI means giving AI a: Physical AI means giving AI a
ur:. Instead of AI that only lives in co: . Instead of AI that only lives in computers (like chatbots), Physical AI systems can move, touch things, and interact with the real world — like robots, self-driving cars, and drones.
ur:Think of it like the difference betwe: Think of it like the difference between a chess-playing computer (only software) and a robot arm that can actually pick up chess pieces (Physical AI).
ur:Physical AI refers to artificial inte: Physical AI refers to artificial intelligence systems that interact with and operate in the physical world. Unlike traditional AI that exists purely in software, Physical AI
ur:in physical form — robots, drones, au: in physical form — robots, drones, autonomous vehicles, and smart devices that can:
ur:their environment through sensors (ca: their environment through sensors (cameras, LiDAR, touch sensors)
ur:about what they sense using AI algori: about what they sense using AI algorithms
ur:upon the world through actuators (mot: upon the world through actuators (motors, grippers, wheels)
ur:The real world is messy and unpredict: The real world is messy and unpredictable. A robot in a warehouse, a car on city streets, or a humanoid helper at home faces challenges that pure software AI never encounters — things move, lighting changes, and mistakes have real consequences.
ur:Physical AI combines smart perception: Physical AI combines smart perception, planning, and precise motion control to handle this complexity.
ur:The physical world is complex, unpred: The physical world is complex, unpredictable, and constantly changing. Physical AI systems must tackle:
ur:: Decisions must be made in milliseco: : Decisions must be made in milliseconds
ur:: Physical systems can cause harm if: : Physical systems can cause harm if they malfunction
ur:: The environment changes, objects mo: : The environment changes, objects move, people behave unpredictably
ur:Physical AI tackles these challenges: Physical AI tackles these challenges by combining advanced perception, intelligent planning, precise control, and continuous learning.
ur:— measure distances using laser pulse: — measure distances using laser pulses (like sonar, but with light)
ur:— feel when the robot is touching som: — feel when the robot is touching something
ur:Robots perceive the world through sen: Robots perceive the world through sensors:

ur:: PID, Model Predictive Control, forc: : PID, Model Predictive Control, force control for manipulation
ur:Robot arms for assembly, welding, and: Robot arms for assembly, welding, and painting
ur:AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots) for w: AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots) for warehouse inventory management
ur:Self-driving cars (Tesla, Waymo) navi: Self-driving cars (Tesla, Waymo) navigating city streets

ur:The main technologies behind Physical: The main technologies behind Physical AI are:

ur:Physical AI is at an inflection point: Physical AI is at an inflection point. Advances in AI, computing power, and hardware are making robots more capable, affordable, and accessible than ever before. Ready to bring AI into the physical world? Let's get started!

ur:The ROS 2 Architecture | Physical AI: The ROS 2 Architecture | Physical AI & Humanoid Robotics
ur:. Just like Windows lets different ap: . Just like Windows lets different apps on your computer talk to each other, ROS 2 lets different parts of a robot's software communicate — the camera software, the motor software, the navigation software, etc.
ur:It doesn&#x27;t matter if they&#x27;r: It doesn't matter if they're written in different languages (Python or C++) or even running on different computers — ROS 2 handles all the communication.
ur:: After completing this chapter, you: : After completing this chapter, you will be able to:
ur:Explain what a Node is and why modula: Explain what a Node is and why modularity is important in robotics.
ur:Describe the Publish-Subscribe patter: Describe the Publish-Subscribe pattern used in ROS 2 Topics.
ur:Understand the role of DDS in robot c: Understand the role of DDS in robot communication.
ur:Identify the different types of ROS 2: Identify the different types of ROS 2 communication patterns.
ur:Despite its name, ROS 2 is not a trad: Despite its name, ROS 2 is not a traditional operating system like Windows or Linux. It is a
ur:— software tools that handle the comp: — software tools that handle the complexity of robot communication.
ur:Robots are complex systems. A humanoi: Robots are complex systems. A humanoid robot might have separate software for vision, leg control, and battery monitoring. ROS 2 lets these programs talk to each other.
ur:framework — a set of software tools a: framework — a set of software tools and libraries that handle the complexity of robot software communication. It uses DDS (Data Distribution Service) as its communication layer, providing decentralized, QoS-configurable messaging between processes.
ur:as a worker in a factory. Each worker: as a worker in a factory. Each worker does one specific job:
ur:(the ROS Graph) and communicate by pa: (the ROS Graph) and communicate by passing notes (messages).
ur:The fundamental unit of software in R: The fundamental unit of software in ROS 2 is the
ur:— a process that performs a specific: — a process that performs a specific task. Each node should represent a single functional unit:
One node controls a camera driver.
ur:One node controls the robot&#x27;s mo: One node controls the robot's motors.
ur:, a network of nodes communicating wi: , a network of nodes communicating with each other.
ur:ROS 2 has three ways for robot parts: ROS 2 has three ways for robot parts to communicate:
ur:— Like a radio broadcast. One sender,: — Like a radio broadcast. One sender, many listeners. Used for continuous data like camera feeds.
ur:— Like a phone call. One node asks a: — Like a phone call. One node asks a question, another answers. Used for one-time requests.
ur:— Like ordering a pizza. You place an: — Like ordering a pizza. You place an order, get progress updates, and receive the result. Used for long tasks.
ur:: Continuous data streams like sensor: : Continuous data streams like sensor readings (LiDAR, camera feeds).
ur:Synchronous. A client sends a request: Synchronous. A client sends a request, a server sends a response.
ur:: One-off actions like &quot;Reset th: : One-off actions like "Reset the odometer" or "Calculate IK solution."
ur:For long-running tasks with progress: For long-running tasks with progress feedback and cancellation support.
ur:: Navigation (&quot;Go to the kitchen: : Navigation ("Go to the kitchen") or complex manipulation.
ur:— structured data packets. For exampl: — structured data packets. For example, to move a robot, you send a   message containing speed and direction. Both sender and receiver must agree on the message format.
ur:defined in files. The message (li: defined in   files. The   message (linear + angular velocity) is the standard for robot motion control. All communicating nodes must agree on the message type.
ur:One of the major improvements in ROS: One of the major improvements in ROS 2 over ROS 1 is the use of
ur:as the underlying communication proto: as the underlying communication protocol — an industry standard used in military, aerospace, and automotive systems.

ur:Physics with Gazebo | Physical AI &am: Physics with Gazebo | Physical AI & Humanoid Robotics
ur:is the industry-standard simulator fo: is the industry-standard simulator for ROS-based robots. It provides a high-fidelity environment for testing Physical AI agents in complex, contact-rich scenarios.

Gazebo simulates the physical world. ROS 2 controls the robot software. They need to communicate — the
ur:Think of it as: Gazebo speaks &quot;p: Think of it as: Gazebo speaks "physics language" and ROS 2 speaks "robot software language". The bridge translates between the two in real-time.
ur:With ROS 2 Humble, the standard simul: With ROS 2 Humble, the standard simulator is
ur:(Fortress or Garden). The provides: (Fortress or Garden). The   provides a bidirectional transport layer — mapping Gazebo's Protobuf-based messages to ROS 2's DDS-based middleware.
ur:When the robot touches the ground or: When the robot touches the ground or picks up an object, the simulator must calculate realistic physics. Two key settings:
ur:— simplified boxes/spheres are faster: — simplified boxes/spheres are faster than complex 3D meshes
ur:— how much the robot&#x27;s feet grip: — how much the robot's feet grip the floor (important for stable walking!)
ur:Always use simplified primitives (sph: Always use simplified primitives (spheres, boxes) for collision shapes — dramatically better performance than complex meshes.
ur:Complex mesh collision shapes can slo: Complex mesh collision shapes can slow simulation to a crawl. Use primitive shapes for all collision geometries.
ur:Gazebo uses the Coulomb friction mode: Gazebo uses the Coulomb friction model:
ur:: Contact stiffness and damping (&quo: : Contact stiffness and damping ("bounciness" of a surface)
ur:— software modules that simulate real: — software modules that simulate real hardware:
ur:— sends out virtual laser beams and m: — sends out virtual laser beams and measures distances
ur:— simulates the robot&#x27;s balance: — simulates the robot's balance and acceleration sensor
ur:Without these plugins, your simulated: Without these plugins, your simulated robot is blind and can't feel anything.
ur:Plugins are C++ libraries extending G: Plugins are C++ libraries extending Gazebo's functionality. For Physical AI, sensors like IMUs and LiDARs are essential for perception and localization.
ur:To enable Gazebo sensors, add refer: To enable Gazebo sensors, add   reference tags to your URDF. These are ignored by standard ROS tools but parsed by Gazebo during model spawning:
ur:: CPU load variations can cause sligh: : CPU load variations can cause slight physics integration differences
ur:: Add Gaussian noise parameters to se: : Add Gaussian noise parameters to sensor output — real sensors are noisy, and your AI must be trained for that
ur:: High friction or mass ratios betwee: : High friction or mass ratios between connected links can cause "jitter"
ur:Add noise to IMU for realistic sim-to: Add noise to IMU for realistic sim-to-real transfer:

ur:Answer: Translates between Gazebo&#x2: Answer: Translates between Gazebo's Protobuf messages and ROS 2's DDS topics — bidirectional data flow
ur:: Why use simplified primitives for c: : Why use simplified primitives for collision tags instead of high-fidelity meshes?
ur:Answer: Dramatically reduces collisio: Answer: Dramatically reduces collision detection computational overhead, keeping simulation near real-time

ur:Simulation Basics | Physical AI &amp;: Simulation Basics | Physical AI & Humanoid Robotics
ur:Simulation is the cornerstone of mode: Simulation is the cornerstone of modern Physical AI development. It serves as a "digital gymnasium" where autonomous agents can learn complex behaviors, test safety boundaries, and iterate on hardware designs without the risks and costs of physical prototypes.
ur:: When you train a robot in a virtual: : When you train a robot in a virtual world, it might not behave the same in the real world. The simulation isn't perfect — friction is slightly different, lighting looks different, and real motors have tiny delays.
ur:A robot learning to walk falls thousa: A robot learning to walk falls thousands of times — in simulation, this costs nothing
ur:You can run 1,000 training sessions s: You can run 1,000 training sessions simultaneously on a GPU
ur:You can test dangerous scenarios safe: You can test dangerous scenarios safely (robot falling off a ledge)
ur:is the discrepancy between performanc: is the discrepancy between performance in simulation vs. the physical world.
ur:: Simplified friction models, rigid b: : Simplified friction models, rigid body assumptions
ur:: Differences in lighting, textures,: : Differences in lighting, textures, sensor noise
ur:: Real-world communication delays ide: : Real-world communication delays idealized in simulation
ur:: Thousands of falls cost nothing in: : Thousands of falls cost nothing in simulation
ur:: 1,000 parallel simulations on a GPU: : 1,000 parallel simulations on a GPU cluster
ur:is the mathematical brain of a simula: is the mathematical brain of a simulation — it calculates how objects move, fall, bounce, and collide. Three main engines are used in robotics:
ur:Accurate robot arm and biology simula: Accurate robot arm and biology simulations
ur:General robotics, free and widely sup: General robotics, free and widely supported
ur:Massive scale, runs thousands of simu: Massive scale, runs thousands of simulations on GPU
ur:Can be slower for complex contact: Can be slower for complex contact
ur:(Unified Robot Description Format) is: (Unified Robot Description Format) is like a robot's
ur:. It&#x27;s an XML file that describe: . It's an XML file that describes:
ur:Each physical part of the robot (link: Each physical part of the robot (links) — the arm, the torso, the hand
ur:How parts connect and move (joints) —: How parts connect and move (joints) — can it rotate? slide? is it fixed?
ur:Without URDF, the simulator doesn&#x2: Without URDF, the simulator doesn't know what the robot looks like or how it moves.
ur:is an XML-based specification describ: is an XML-based specification describing geometry, kinematics, and dynamics.
ur:: Rigid bodies with mass, inertia, vi: : Rigid bodies with mass, inertia, visual and collision properties
ur:: Connections (revolute, prismatic, f: : Connections (revolute, prismatic, fixed) defining degrees of freedom
ur:is a clever trick: during training, r: is a clever trick: during training, randomly change things like floor friction, lighting, and object weights. This forces the robot to learn a robust strategy that works in many different conditions — including the messy real world.
ur:bridges the Sim-to-Real gap by varyin: bridges the Sim-to-Real gap by varying physical parameters (friction, mass, lighting, textures) during training. The agent learns a policy that generalizes to the real world by treating it as just another variation of the training distribution.

ur:Answer: Randomizing simulation parame: Answer: Randomizing simulation parameters so the AI model generalizes to the real world

ur:Answer: Visual only defines appearanc: Answer: Visual only defines appearance. Collision defines physics interaction; inertial defines mass distribution — both essential for accurate simulation

ur:Whole-Body Control | Physical AI &amp: Whole-Body Control | Physical AI & Humanoid Robotics
ur:Whole-Body Control (WBC): The Orchest: Whole-Body Control (WBC): The Orchestration of Motion
ur:Humanoid robots have 20 to 50+ joints: Humanoid robots have 20 to 50+ joints — that's a lot of moving parts!
ur:is the &quot;choreographer&quot; that: is the "choreographer" that makes sure every joint works together at the same time.
ur:Think of it like an orchestra conduct: Think of it like an orchestra conductor: the conductor ensures the violins, drums, and flutes all play in harmony. WBC does the same for a robot's joints, making sure the arms, legs, and torso move together without falling over.
ur:— keeping balance is always the top p: — keeping balance is always the top priority
ur:Humanoid robots are highly redundant: Humanoid robots are highly redundant systems, often possessing 20 to 50+ degrees of freedom (DoF). To perform complex tasks while maintaining balance, they require a control architecture that can coordinate every joint simultaneously. This is the role of
ur:. The most important tasks are done f: . The most important tasks are done first. If there's still room to move (extra degrees of freedom), lower-priority tasks are done too.
ur:WBC leverages the robot&#x27;s redund: WBC leverages the robot's redundancy by defining a hierarchy of tasks. High-priority tasks are executed first, and lower-priority tasks are satisfied using the remaining degrees of freedom (the
ur:Under the hood, WBC solves a math puz: Under the hood, WBC solves a math puzzle every millisecond:
ur:&quot;What torque should each motor a: "What torque should each motor apply right now?"

ur:When a robot stands, it pushes down o: When a robot stands, it pushes down on the floor and the floor pushes back. WBC carefully controls
ur:If a foot pushes sideways too hard, i: If a foot pushes sideways too hard, it will slip — just like you sliding on ice. WBC keeps the forces in a "safe zone" to prevent slipping.
ur:Imagine a cone drawn around the foot&: Imagine a cone drawn around the foot's contact point. As long as the force stays inside the cone, the foot won't slip. WBC always keeps forces inside this cone.
ur:A key feature of WBC is its ability t: A key feature of WBC is its ability to optimize
ur:. By distributing weight across feet: . By distributing weight across feet (or hands during multi-contact), the robot can maximize its stability margin.

ur:leaves this cone, the robot&#x27;s fo: leaves this cone, the robot's foot will slip. WBC ensures forces stay within the "safety zone."

ur:: When a foot lifts off the ground, t: : When a foot lifts off the ground, the math changes suddenly — this needs careful handling
ur:: If the model says a joint weighs X: : If the model says a joint weighs X but it actually weighs Y, the robot can fall
ur:: QP solvers must converge in under 2: : QP solvers must converge in under 2ms to maintain stable control at 500Hz+.

ur:In the era of Physical AI, humanoid r: In the era of Physical AI, humanoid robots transition from static models to dynamic agents. Understanding the mathematical foundation of their movement is the first step toward achieving fluid, human-like motion.
ur:A humanoid robot is like a puppet wit: A humanoid robot is like a puppet with a body (pelvis), arms, and legs. Each part can move, and the connections between parts are called
ur:Unlike a fixed robot arm bolted to th: Unlike a fixed robot arm bolted to the floor, a humanoid has a
ur:— the body can move and rotate freely: — the body can move and rotate freely in space. This adds complexity: 6 extra degrees of freedom (3 for position, 3 for rotation).
ur:Humanoid robots are modeled as multi-: Humanoid robots are modeled as multi-body systems with a
ur:(usually pelvis) having 6 degrees of: (usually pelvis) having 6 degrees of freedom (DoF) in space, plus multiple serial chains (limbs).

ur:Creating stable, dynamic walking is t: Creating stable, dynamic walking is the holy grail of humanoid robotics. This chapter covers the theoretical foundations and practical implementations of bipedal locomotion.
ur:Walking is actually a controlled fall: Walking is actually a controlled fall. When you walk, you lean forward and catch yourself with each step. A robot must:
ur:This is much harder than wheeled robo: This is much harder than wheeled robots, which are naturally stable.

ur:Over the last few years, a series of: Over the last few years, a series of AI models have made robots dramatically smarter. Think of it like generations of smartphones — each one built on the previous, getting more capable.
ur:Here&#x27;s the family tree of robot: Here's the family tree of robot AI:
ur:Combined vision + language + action i: Combined vision + language + action in one model
ur:To build a general-purpose robot, we: To build a general-purpose robot, we must understand the "lineage" of its brain. Between 2023 and 2025, a series of breakthrough models redefined what is possible in embodied AI.
ur:was Google DeepMind&#x27;s first big: was Google DeepMind's first big experiment: can we train a transformer on robot data and make it useful?
ur:The answer was yes — but it had limit: The answer was yes — but it had limits. It was like a student who studied one textbook very well but struggled when anything was slightly different.
ur:❌ No deep language understanding — co: ❌ No deep language understanding — couldn't reason about
ur:was one of the first large-scale appl: was one of the first large-scale applications of transformers to robot control.
ur:A FiLM-conditioned EfficientNet visua: A FiLM-conditioned EfficientNet visual encoder coupled with a standard Transformer.
ur:It demonstrated that large-scale data: It demonstrated that large-scale data collection (130k episodes) could lead to robust performance across many tasks.
ur:It lacked the deep semantic reasoning: It lacked the deep semantic reasoning of a Large Language Model (LLM).
ur:was a huge leap. Instead of building: was a huge leap. Instead of building a robot AI from scratch, Google plugged sensor data directly into a massive language model (PaLM).
ur:It&#x27;s like hiring a genius who al: It's like hiring a genius who already knows everything about the world, and just teaching them to control a robot. The genius's background knowledge made them much better at tasks.
ur:was a massive leap: it directly injec: was a massive leap: it directly injected visual and sensor data into the
ur:It showed that a model trained on tex: It showed that a model trained on text and images could perform robotic tasks
ur:by leveraging its broad world knowled: by leveraging its broad world knowledge.
ur:introduced a clever idea: treat robot: introduced a clever idea: treat robot movements like words in a sentence.
ur:Instead of having a special &quot;rob: Instead of having a special "robot output", RT-2 converts joint movements into tokens (just like words are tokens in language models). This means the robot can use the full power of transformers to "think" about what to do next.
ur:Result: a robot that could understand: Result: a robot that could understand context and even generalize to new situations.
ur:introduced the term &quot;VLA.&quot;: introduced the term "VLA." It simplified the pipeline by using
ur:is the most important model for most: is the most important model for most people because it's
— anyone can download and use it.
ur:Think of it as the &quot;Llama for Ro: Think of it as the "Llama for Robotics": a powerful model that researchers and companies can take, adapt, and improve for their own robot.
ur:⚡ Can be fine-tuned on your specific: ⚡ Can be fine-tuned on your specific robot in hours
ur:📦 Only 7B parameters — runs on consum: 📦 Only 7B parameters — runs on consumer hardware with quantization
ur:(Stanford, Berkeley, TRI) represents: (Stanford, Berkeley, TRI) represents the current pinnacle of accessible, high-performance VLA research. It is designed to be the "Llama for Robotics."
ur:framework. It fuses two visual backbo: framework. It fuses two visual backbones to capture both semantic and spatial details:
ur:Unlike its predecessors, OpenVLA is o: Unlike its predecessors, OpenVLA is optimized for
ur:, you can adapt the 7B parameter mode: , you can adapt the 7B parameter model to a new robot embodiment in just a few hours.
ur:Using OpenVLA is surprisingly straigh: Using OpenVLA is surprisingly straightforward. You give it a camera image and a text command, and it returns what the robot should do next — encoded as a list of numbers for each joint.
ur:The key challenge is doing this fast: The key challenge is doing this fast enough (many times per second) while the robot is moving.
ur:To use a VLA model, you typically pas: To use a VLA model, you typically pass the current camera observation and the goal instruction. The model returns a normalized vector of actions.
ur:Why do we tokenize actions?\nBy turni:
Why do we tokenize actions?
By turning joint angles (e.g., 45.2 degrees) into a discrete token (e.g.,  ), the model handles robotics exactly like language. This allows the transformer to use its native
ur:to find correlations between the visu: to find correlations between the visual feature of a "mug" and the action token for "grasping."

ur:— they can simulate &quot;what would: — they can simulate "what would happen if I did X?" before actually doing it.
ur:Think of it like a chess player who t: Think of it like a chess player who thinks several moves ahead. Before picking up a glass, the robot "imagines" three ways to do it and picks the safest one.
ur:Even more exciting: robots will learn: Even more exciting: robots will learn by
ur:— exploring their environment like a: — exploring their environment like a curious child, without needing humans to label every example.
ur:The next generation of robot brains w: The next generation of robot brains won't just map vision and language to actions. They will maintain latent
ur:Before moving its arm, a 2025 humanoi: Before moving its arm, a 2025 humanoid "imagines" the potential outcome of three different trajectories in its latent space, selecting the one with the highest safety and efficiency score.
ur:Robots will no longer require million: Robots will no longer require millions of human-labeled trajectories. Instead, they will engage in "autonomous play" (e.g., Google DeepMind's
ur:evolutions), learning physics, fricti: evolutions), learning physics, friction, and object persistence through pure exploration.
ur:Future robots won&#x27;t just hear wh: Future robots won't just hear what you say — they'll understand
ur:If you say &quot;careful!&quot; with: If you say "careful!" with panic in your voice, the robot will slow down and be gentler. If you seem rushed, it will work faster. If you reach for something, it might hand it to you before you even ask.
ur:— robots that respond to human emotio: — robots that respond to human emotions.
ur:As robots move into homes and hospita: As robots move into homes and hospitals, "Action" tokens are expanding to include
ur:Future VLAs will process vocal tone a: Future VLAs will process vocal tone and facial micro-expressions. If a user says "Careful!" with a panicked tone, the VLA doesn't just lower its speed; it adjusts its impedance control to a "soft" safety mode.
ur:Using historical context, a robot wil: Using historical context, a robot will predict human intent. If you reach for a coffee cup, the robot's VLA anticipates the need for the creamer and positions it within your reach before you ask.
ur:3. The Humanoid Explosion: Project GR: 3. The Humanoid Explosion: Project GR00T and Beyond
ur:2025 is the year humanoid robots go m: 2025 is the year humanoid robots go mainstream. Companies like NVIDIA, Tesla, and Boston Dynamics are racing to build robots that:

ur:Learn from watching human motion capt: Learn from watching human motion capture videos

ur:: Robots &quot;live&quot; 10,000 year: : Robots "live" 10,000 years of virtual experience before ever touching the physical world
ur:: A model trained on a factory arm ca: : A model trained on a factory arm can be transferred to a kitchen robot — it understands "stirring" regardless of the hardware
ur:: Robots will respond to continuous a: : Robots will respond to continuous audio and visual input, not just one-off text commands
ur:, robots &quot;live&quot; 10,000 year: , robots "live" 10,000 years of experience in 24 hours of GPU compute before ever touching the physical world.
ur:A VLA learned on a collaborative arm: A VLA learned on a collaborative arm in a factory can be "downloaded" into a kitchen robot. The model understands the high-level concept of "stirring" regardless of the hardware's specific dimensions.
ur:Moving beyond text prompts to continu: Moving beyond text prompts to continuous, streaming audio-visual feedback.
ur:As robots get smarter, keeping them s: As robots get smarter, keeping them safe and aligned with human values becomes critical.
ur:— even if they weren&#x27;t explicitl: — even if they weren't explicitly programmed
ur:— humans must be able to understand: — humans must be able to understand

ur:With great intelligence comes the nee: With great intelligence comes the need for hardcoded ethical constraints. 2025 research from

ur:Tools that allow engineers to &quot;s: Tools that allow engineers to "see" why a VLA chose a specific action (e.g., "The model prioritized the glass's fragility over movement speed").
ur:As VLAs become more autonomous, the l: As VLAs become more autonomous, the line between "tool" and "agent" blurs. The 2025 AI Safety Summits focus heavily on ensuring robots remain aligned with human values even as they learn and adapt independently.

ur:The robots of the future will not be: The robots of the future will not be programmed; they will be
ur:. Through Vision-Language-Action mode: . Through Vision-Language-Action models, we have given them the eyes to see our world and the language to understand our intent. The next step is giving them the wisdom to act within it.

ur:Imagine a robot that can look at a ki: Imagine a robot that can look at a kitchen, understand you when you say
ur:, and then actually do it — without y: , and then actually do it — without you programming every single step. That's what
ur:VLA is like giving a robot a: VLA is like giving a robot a
ur:Before VLA, robots needed to be progr: Before VLA, robots needed to be programmed for each specific task. Now one model can handle many different tasks, just like a person can.
ur:Welcome to the era of the: Welcome to the era of the
ur:. For decades, robotics was defined b: . For decades, robotics was defined by specialized controllers—finite state machines designed for single tasks in controlled environments. The emergence of
ur:models has flipped this script, provi: models has flipped this script, providing robots with a unified "brain" capable of reasoning across modalities.
ur:A VLA model is an AI system that take: A VLA model is an AI system that takes three things as input:
ur:— exact movements for every joint and: — exact movements for every joint and motor.
ur:It&#x27;s like a GPS for robot moveme: It's like a GPS for robot movement: you tell it the destination, it figures out every step to get there.
ur:A Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model: A Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model is a generative foundation model that consumes visual observations and natural language instructions to directly output action tokens for a robot.
ur:In the physical AI landscape of 2024-: In the physical AI landscape of 2024-2025, we are moving away from scripting every joint movement. Instead, we provide a semantic goal, and the VLA computes the high-level trajectory by "reading" the world and "writing" the actions.
ur:VLAs are the evolution of Vision-Lang: VLAs are the evolution of Vision-Language Models (VLMs). While a VLM might describe a scene (e.g., "A red apple on a table"), a VLA understands how to
ur:with it (e.g., &quot;Pick up the appl: with it (e.g., "Pick up the apple and place it in the basket").
ur:: Looks at the scene, understands the: : Looks at the scene, understands the goal, decides "move hand here"
ur:: Makes the motors actually move safe: : Makes the motors actually move safely and smoothly
ur:VLA thinks slowly (5–20 times per sec: VLA thinks slowly (5–20 times per second), while the low-level controller acts fast (500–1000 times per second). They work as a team.
ur:In modern Physical AI architecture, w: In modern Physical AI architecture, we distinguish between high-level reasoning and low-level control:
ur:Understands &quot;what&quot; to do an: Understands "what" to do and "how" to move in space. It bridges semantics (language) with spatial awareness (vision). It typically operates at frequencies of 5Hz to 20Hz.
ur:Handles high-frequency signal process: Handles high-frequency signal processing (PID, Impedance control, Whole-Body Control) to ensure the hardware follows the VLA's path safely and smoothly at 500Hz to 1kHz.
ur:The &quot;Robot Brain&quot; isn&#x27;: The "Robot Brain" isn't just a metaphor. By scaling parameters (7B to 100B+), these models represent a centralized cognitive architecture that can be shared across different robot embodiments—from industrial arms to humanoid explorers.
ur:Recent breakthroughs have made VLA mo: Recent breakthroughs have made VLA models practical:
ur:: Like how ChatGPT can help with writ: : Like how ChatGPT can help with writing, coding, and math — one VLA can handle picking, placing, pouring, and more
ur:: Models learn from data collected by: : Models learn from data collected by 22+ different robot types, making them more general
ur:: The robot can handle objects it has: : The robot can handle objects it has never seen before, just by reasoning about what they look like
ur:(Brohan et al., 2023) marked the tran: (Brohan et al., 2023) marked the transition from lab experiments to deployable generalists. Key breakthroughs include:
ur:Treating robot movements (joint angle: Treating robot movements (joint angles, end-effector positions) exactly like words in a sentence. This allows actions to benefit from the powerful attention mechanisms of transformers.
ur:Training one model on data from hundr: Training one model on data from hundreds of different robot types, such as the
ur:, which aggregates data from 22+ diff: , which aggregates data from 22+ different robot types.
ur:The ability to manipulate objects or: The ability to manipulate objects or perform tasks the robot has never seen before based purely on visual-linguistic reasoning inherited from web-scale pretraining.
ur:VLA models are powerful but come with: VLA models are powerful but come with real challenges:
ur:: Running a massive AI model fast eno: : Running a massive AI model fast enough for real-time robot control is very hard
ur:: We have tons of text and images on: : We have tons of text and images on the internet, but very little
ur:: We need the robot to never do somet: : We need the robot to never do something dangerous, even when confused
ur:Running a 7B+ parameter model fast en: Running a 7B+ parameter model fast enough for real-time control requires significant optimization (quantization, specialized hardware).
ur:While text/image data is nearly infin: While text/image data is nearly infinite, high-quality "Action" data (annotated robot trajectories) remains the primary bottleneck for Physical AI.
ur:Ensuring a large transformer doesn&#x: Ensuring a large transformer doesn't execute a high-torque action that could damage the environment or itself when faced with an ambiguous prompt.

ur:is like a worker in a factory. Each w: is like a worker in a factory. Each worker does one specific job — one worker might read sensor data, another might drive the motors. They communicate by passing notes (called
ur:You can run many workers at once, and: You can run many workers at once, and they all work together to make the robot function.
ur:A ROS 2 node is a process that uses t: A ROS 2 node is a process that uses the rclpy or rclcpp client libraries to participate in the ROS 2 graph. Nodes communicate via:
ur:ROS 2 nodes use managed lifecycle (rc: ROS 2 nodes use managed lifecycle (rclcpp_lifecycle) for deterministic startup/shutdown. The DDS middleware (FastDDS, CycloneDDS) handles discovery via SPDP/SEDP and QoS negotiation.
